---
title: "Windows first on Grub 2 - Karmic"
date: 2010-02-07
forum: General Help
---

### Post by el canadiano on 2010-02-07
Hey,

I was wondering how I can make Windows XP the first and default option on my Grub 2. I use Ubuntu 9.10 through a semi-dedicated partition on my second hard drive, if that helps. I'm pretty sure I use Grub 2 just cause I have a grub.cfg file, and here it is.

```
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s /boot/grub/grubenv ]; then
  have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
fi
insmod ext2
set root=(hd1,5)
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't
    # understand terminal_output
    terminal gfxterm
  fi
fi
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/white
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic" {
        recordfail=1
        if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi
	set quiet=1
	insmod ext2
	set root=(hd1,5)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792 ro   quiet splash
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)" {
        recordfail=1
        if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi
	insmod ext2
	set root=(hd1,5)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792 ro single 
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition (on /dev/sda1)" {
	insmod ntfs
	set root=(hd0,1)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 60648b20648af7d4
	drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
	chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
```

### Post by Brandon Williams on 2010-02-07
How important is it for Windows to come first in the menu order? If it would be enough for it to be the default selection, then open /etc/default/grub in your favorite text editor (using sudo) and change this line:
```
GRUB_DEFAULT=0
```
to this:
```
GRUB_DEFAULT="Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition (on /dev/sda1)"
```
After making this (or any) change, you have to run 'sudo update-grub' to apply the changes to the grub.cfg file.

If it's really important to you that the Windows OS come first in the menu, the easiest way to do it would be to rename /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober so that it will be run before /etc/grub.d/10_linux. This would do it:
```
sudo dpkg-divert  --local --divert /etc/grub.d/09_os-prober --rename --add /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober
```
By using dpkg-divert, the change will not be over-written if grub is updated in the future. Renaming 30_os-prober to 09_os-prober will cause the os-prober (responsible for writing out the Windows OS menu entry) to be run earlier than 10_linux. If you want to revert this change, run:
```
sudo dpkg-divert --rename --remove /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober
```

If you rename /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober, you will not have to change /etc/default/grub, but you will still need to run 'sudo update-grub' to apply the change.

More information about working with grub2 can be found [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275) and [here](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2).

### Post by el canadiano on 2010-02-07
The main reason I want Windows first (default will do it, I guess) is because my mother is the main user of this computer, and she uses Windows the most.

---

### Post by OrangeCrate on 2010-02-07
As an alternative, you can use startupmanager to control which OS boots first. It's easy...

```
sudo apt-get install startupmanager
```

It will then be listed under System > Administration > StartUp Manager

---

### Post by itagomo on 2010-08-22
Actually you could edit the grub.cfg according to what do you want to be the first to boot or on the list.

just cut this text:
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition (on /dev/sda1)" {
	insmod ntfs
	set root=(hd0,1)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 60648b20648af7d4
	drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
	chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

and paste it on top of this:
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic" {
        recordfail=1
        if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi
	set quiet=1
	insmod ext2
	set root=(hd1,5)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792 ro   quiet splash
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.31-14-generic (recovery mode)" {
        recordfail=1
        if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi
	insmod ext2
	set root=(hd1,5)
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.31-14-generic root=UUID=fb4a076e-962e-4af2-9364-7509b5f6c792 ro single 
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.31-14-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
